message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-9 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use "python -m pytest"/"source .venv/bin/activate"; you may run gh for CI claims.

FINDING P9-REG-2 (medium, FIXREG8): K-OBS-2 default=str on the tracker artifact diverges from persist_trail(), which still raises on the same hashable-but-non-JSON payload
Location: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:756-758 (_log_trail json.dumps default=str) vs. pipeline.py:240-243 (StudyResult.persist_trail json.dumps with no default)
Claim: K-OBS-2 added default=str to the tracker-artifact serialization so a payload carrying a hashable-but-non-JSON leaf (bytes/datetime/Enum — all accepted by ProvenanceTrail.record) no longer orphans a temp file or silently drops the #G-009 artifact. But StudyResult.persist_trail() — the documented durable, re-verifiable audit trail that 'round-trips through from_records / verify_records' — still calls json.dumps WITHOUT default=str. So the very same trail that _log_trail writes successfully (artifact attaches green, values silently stringified) makes persist_trail raise TypeError. The two serialization paths for one trail now disagree: the tracker artifact and the on-disk audit trail are not the same bytes, and the path operators are told to use for durable audit is the one that breaks. Additionally, default=str coerces a bytes leaf to its repr (e.g. b'...' -> "b'...'"), so any sensitive bytes that previously errored-out (and were never written) now land in the tracker artifact in stringified form.
Why real: Reproduced directly: ProvenanceTrail().record('x','msg', when=datetime(...), blob=b'secret-bytes') is accepted; entries_to_records then json.dumps (persist_trail path) raises 'Object of type datetime is not JSON serializable', while json.dumps(..., default=str) (the _log_trail path) succeeds producing payload {"when":"2020-01-01 00:00...", blob stringified}. The fix hardened one of two serialization sites for the identical input, leaving an inconsistency and a broken primary persistence path.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

226	    def persist_trail(self, path: str | Path) -> Path:
227	        """Write the hash-chained provenance trail to ``path`` as JSON and return it (#F-045).
228	
229	        ``run_study`` keeps the trail only in memory (``self.provenance``); a caller that needs a
230	        durable, re-verifiable audit trail across process exit must persist it. The written
231	        records round-trip through :func:`~assay_engine.provenance.from_records` and re-check via
232	        :func:`~assay_engine.provenance.verify_records`. Call ``tracker.log_artifact`` on the
233	        returned path to attach it to the experiment run.
234	        """
235	        import json

237	        from assay_engine.provenance import entries_to_records

741	            def _log_trail(t: ExperimentTracker, rid: str) -> None:
742	                import json
743	                import os
744	                import tempfile
745	
746	                from assay_engine.provenance import entries_to_records
747	
748	                # Serialize FIRST, with a JSON-safe fallback (#K-OBS-2). freeze() accepts any
749	                # HASHABLE leaf, so a payload may carry a hashable-but-non-JSON value (bytes,
750	                # datetime, Enum) — e.g. in gate evidence. The old code called json.dump INSIDE the
751	                # NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) block BEFORE `path = fh.name`, so a TypeError
752	                # there (a) orphaned the temp file (cleanup unreachable — path never assigned) AND
753	                # (b) was swallowed by track() so the #G-009 artifact silently never attached.
754	                # Building the string up front with default=str means serialization can't leak a
755	                # file and the artifact still attaches even with such values.
756	                payload = json.dumps(
757	                    list(entries_to_records(list(trail.entries))), indent=2, default=str
758	                )
759	                fh = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
760	                    "w", suffix="_provenance.json", delete=False, encoding="utf-8"
761	                )
762	                # Clean up the scratch file after the tracker has copied it into its store, so a
763	                # tracked run does not leak a temp file (with provenance contents) on every call
764	                # (#H-013). `fh.name` is always assigned (the file exists once created), so the
765	                # finally cleanup is always reachable. The tracker copies synchronously.
766	                try:
767	                    fh.write(payload)
768	                    fh.close()
769	                    t.log_artifact(rid, fh.name)
770	                finally:
771	                    try:
772	                        os.unlink(fh.name)
773	                    except OSError:
774	                        pass
775	
776	            track(_log_trail)
777	        ok = True
778	    except Exception as exc:
779	        # Record a terminal failure entry BEFORE re-raising so the abort reason is recoverable

content.text: Both locations are confirmed. The finding's core claim is accurate: `_log_trail` (line 756-758) uses `json.dumps(..., default=str)` while `persist_trail` (line 240-243) uses `json.dumps(...)` with no default. Let me verify the reproduction claim about `ProvenanceTrail.record` accepting these leaf types and `entries_to_records` output.

116	    def record(self, kind: str, summary: str, **payload: Any) -> ProvenanceEntry:
117	        """Append one entry. Returns it. The only way to add to the trail. Thread-safe."""
118	        if not kind:
119	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance entry kind must be non-empty")
120	        with self._lock:
121	            return self._record_locked(kind, summary, payload)
122	
123	    def _record_locked(
124	        self, kind: str, summary: str, payload: Mapping[str, Any]
125	    ) -> ProvenanceEntry:
126	        seq = len(self._entries)
127	        prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
128	        try:
129	            instant = self._clock()
130	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the clock is an injected dependency
131	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance clock raised: {exc}") from None
132	        if not isinstance(instant, _dt.datetime):
133	            raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a datetime")
134	        try:
135	            # utcoffset()/astimezone()/isoformat() can raise on a hostile datetime subclass or an
136	            # out-of-range value — keep the typed-error contract (#96).
137	            if instant.tzinfo is None or instant.utcoffset() is None:
138	                raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a timezone-aware datetime")
139	            timestamp = instant.astimezone(_UTC).isoformat()
140	        except ProvenanceError:
141	            raise
142	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
143	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance timestamp could not be normalized: {exc}") from None
144	        entry_hash = _digest(seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp, prev_hash, self._secret)
145	        try:
146	            entry = ProvenanceEntry(
147	                seq=seq,
148	                kind=kind,
149	                summary=summary,
150	                payload=payload,
151	                timestamp=timestamp,
152	                prev_hash=prev_hash,
153	                entry_hash=entry_hash,
154	            )
155	        except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
156	            raise ProvenanceError(
157	                f"provenance entry for {kind!r} could not be frozen ({exc})"
158	            ) from None
159	        self._entries.append(entry)
160	        return entry
161	
162	    def as_recorder(self) -> Callable[[Any], None]:
163	        """A :data:`gates.ProvenanceRecorder` bound to this trail (records GateDecisions).
164	
165	        Reads each attribute exactly once and defensively, so a booby-trapped decision object
166	        (a property that raises) surfaces a typed ``ProvenanceError`` rather than a raw error.
167	        """
168	
169	        def _record(decision: Any) -> None:
170	            try:
171	                gate = getattr(decision, "gate", None)
172	                approved = bool(getattr(decision, "approved", False))
173	                reason = getattr(decision, "reason", "")
174	                evidence = dict(getattr(decision, "evidence", {}) or {})
175	            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the decision is on the trust boundary
176	                raise ProvenanceError(
177	                    f"could not read gate decision for provenance: {exc}"
178	                ) from None
179	            self.record(
180	                "gate",
181	                f"gate {gate!r}: {'approved' if approved else 'blocked'}",
182	                gate=gate,
183	                approved=approved,
184	                reason=reason,
185	                evidence=evidence,
186	            )
187	
188	        return _record
189	
190	    @property
191	    def entries(self) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
192	        return tuple(self._entries)
193	
194	    @property
195	    def head(self) -> str:
196	        """The current chain head (last entry_hash, or the genesis if empty)."""
197	        return self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
198	
199	    def __len__(self) -> int:
200	        return len(self._entries)
201	
202	    def verify(self) -> None:
203	        """Recompute the chain; raise :class:`ProvenanceError` on any inconsistency."""
204	        verify_records(self._entries, secret=self._secret)
205	
206	    def to_records(self) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
207	        """Serialize to plain dicts (for a persistent store); re-checkable via :func:`verify_records`."""
208	        return entries_to_records(self._entries)
209	
210	
211	def entries_to_records(
212	    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
213	) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
214	    """Serialize loose entries (e.g. ``StudyResult.provenance``) to plain dicts (#F-045).
215	
216	    The same shape :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` produces, so a caller holding only the
217	    returned entries — not the trail object — can still persist a re-checkable trail.
218	
219	    Keying is NOT embedded (pass 5, #H-017): a keyed (HMAC) trail and an unkeyed (SHA-256) trail
220	    serialize identically — the records carry no key material and no trusted algorithm marker (one
221	    would be spoofable since it is not itself part of the per-entry digest). The verifier must
222	    therefore know out-of-band whether the trail was keyed and supply the same ``secret`` to
223	    :func:`from_records` / :func:`verify_records`; that is deliberate key management, not a gap —
224	    ``verify_records`` fails closed under the wrong/absent secret.
225	    """
226	    return tuple(
227	        {
228	            "seq": e.seq,
229	            "kind": e.kind,
230	            "summary": e.summary,
231	            # deep-thaw nested FrozenDict/tuple values so the record is plain + JSON-serializable
232	            # (#F-045): dict(e.payload) only shallow-converts and leaves nested FrozenDicts (e.g.
233	            # the baseline 'determinism' map) which json.dumps cannot encode.
234	            "payload": cast("dict[str, Any]", unfreeze(e.payload)),
235	            "timestamp": e.timestamp,
236	            "prev_hash": e.prev_hash,
237	            "entry_hash": e.entry_hash,
238	        }
239	        for e in entries
240	    )
241	
242	
243	def verify_records(
244	    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
245	    *,
246	    secret: bytes | None = None,
247	) -> None:
248	    """Verify a sequence of entries forms an intact chain (order, linkage, content).
249	
250	    Pass the same ``secret`` the trail was keyed with; an unkeyed trail verifies with
251	    ``secret=None``. A keyed trail will not verify under the wrong/absent secret.
252	    """
253	    prev = _GENESIS
254	    for i, e in enumerate(entries):
255	        if e.seq != i:
256	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} has seq {e.seq} (reordered or removed)")
257	        if e.prev_hash != prev:
258	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} prev_hash breaks the chain")
259	        recomputed = _digest(e.seq, e.kind, e.summary, e.payload, e.timestamp, e.prev_hash, secret)
260	        if not hmac.compare_digest(recomputed, e.entry_hash):
261	            raise ProvenanceError(
262	                f"provenance entry {i} content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)"
263	            )
264	        prev = e.entry_hash
265	
266	
267	def from_records(
268	    records: "list[Mapping[str, Any]] | tuple[Mapping[str, Any], ...]",
269	    *,
270	    secret: bytes | None = None,
271	) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
272	    """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
273	    _required = ("seq", "kind", "summary", "payload", "timestamp", "prev_hash", "entry_hash")
274	    rebuilt: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
275	    for i, r in enumerate(records):

content.text: Now let me verify the actual reproduction the finding claims. The key question: does `record` accept bytes/datetime in payload, and does `entries_to_records` -> `json.dumps` (no default) actually raise for those? Let me run it directly.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import json, datetime
from assay_engine.provenance import ProvenanceTrail, entries_to_records
t = ProvenanceTrail()
t.record('x','msg', when=datetime.datetime(2020,1,1,tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), blob=b'secret-bytes')
recs = list(entries_to_records(list(t.entries)))
try:
    json.dumps(recs, indent=2)
    print('persist_trail path: SUCCEEDED (no default)')
except TypeError as e:
    print('persist_trail path RAISED:', e)
print('---')
print('_log_trail path:', json.dumps(recs, indent=2, default=str)[:200])
"

      "blob": "b'secret-bytes'"
    },
    "timestamp": "2026-06-17T16:01:18.0142

content.text:
The reproduction holds exactly as the finding claims. The two serialization paths genuinely diverge: `_log_trail` succeeds with `default=str` (stringifying datetime and bytes), while `persist_trail` raises `TypeError`.

Now I need to assess whether this is a *real production defect* or out-of-scope/not-real. The critical question: can a real payload actually carry a bytes/datetime leaf in production? The finding asserts "all accepted by ProvenanceTrail.record" and "e.g. in gate evidence." Let me check whether production code paths ever put such leaves into the trail, and whether `from_records` round-trip would even work with the stringified values (i.e., is the `default=str` path itself actually a correct/durable serialization?).

content.content:
275	    for i, r in enumerate(records):
276	        missing = [k for k in _required if k not in r]
277	        if missing:
278	            # A malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a
279	            # raw KeyError, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file
280	            # covers this case too (pass 4, #G-012).
281	            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance record {i} is missing field(s) {missing}")
282	        rebuilt.append(

content.text:
Now let me critically examine the `default=str` path. The finding implies `_log_trail`'s `default=str` is a "fix" producing a green artifact, while `persist_trail` is "broken." But there's a crucial wrinkle: `verify_records` recomputes `_digest` over the *original* in-memory payload (with the real bytes/datetime). After `default=str` stringifies, would `from_records` round-trip and `verify`?

If the `default=str` path produces records that can NOT round-trip and verify, then `_log_trail`'s output is itself NOT a valid re-verifiable trail — it's just a lossy display artifact. That would change the framing entirely. Let me test.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate 2>/dev/null; python -c "
import json, datetime
from assay_engine.provenance import ProvenanceTrail, entries_to_records, from_records
t = ProvenanceTrail()
t.record('x','msg', when=datetime.datetime(2020,1,1,tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), blob=b'secret-bytes')
recs = list(entries_to_records(list(t.entries)))
# round-trip the default=str (lossy) JSON like _log_trail wrote it
written = json.dumps(recs, indent=2, default=str)
reloaded = json.loads(written)
try:
    from_records(reloaded)
    print('default=str artifact round-trips + verifies: TRUE')
except Exception as e:
    print('default=str artifact FAILS verify on reload:', type(e).__name__, e)
"
input.description: Test if default=str artifact verifies on reload

content.content: default=str artifact FAILS verify on reload: ProvenanceError provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)

content.text:
This is the decisive finding. The `default=str` artifact written by `_log_trail` is itself NOT a re-verifiable audit trail — when reloaded via `from_records`/`verify_records`, it fails the hash check because the stringified values no longer match the original digest. So `_log_trail` produces a green-but-broken artifact regardless.

Now the central question for the finding's validity: does any real production code path actually put bytes/datetime/Enum into the trail payload? If nothing in production ever does this, the divergence is unreachable. Let me check what production actually records.

Let me check what `payload` contains at line 315 and whether gate evidence could realistically carry datetime/bytes/Enum.

content.text:
The production internal calls all pass JSON-safe scalars (strings, names, numbers, bools). The realistic external vector is gate `evidence` — a caller-supplied dict via `as_recorder`. The finding's framing rests on that being reachable with non-JSON leaves. That is plausible since evidence is operator-supplied.

But I now have the decisive technical point that REFUTES the finding's central claim. The finding asserts:

> the tracker artifact and the on-disk audit trail are not the same bytes, and the path operators are told to use for durable audit is the one that breaks ... [persist_trail] makes the very same trail that _log_trail writes successfully

The finding frames `persist_trail`'s `TypeError` as the *defect* and `_log_trail`'s success as the *correct behavior to match*. But I've shown the opposite: `_log_trail`'s `default=str` output is a **non-verifiable, corrupted** artifact — it fails `from_records`/`verify_records`, the very round-trip contract `persist_trail` documents. `persist_trail` failing closed (raising `TypeError`) on an input that cannot produce a verifiable trail is the *correct* behavior for a durable audit trail, not a defect. The two paths SHOULD diverge: a display/observability artifact (lossy, best-effort) vs. a durable cryptographic audit trail (must be exact-or-fail-closed).

Let me confirm `_log_trail` is explicitly best-effort (track swallows errors) and that there's no documented requirement that the two produce identical bytes.

content.content:
317	    def track(fn: Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]) -> None:
318	        # observability is best-effort: a tracker failure is recorded but never aborts a run.
319	        if tracker is None or run_id is None:
320	            return
321	        try:
322	            fn(tracker, run_id)
323	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — tracker is an external, optional backend
324	            trail.record("tracking_error", f"experiment tracker call failed: {exc}")
325	            # Emit a structured warning too (#K-OBS-2): a tracker call failing used to be recorded
326	            # ONLY to the trail, so e.g. a provenance-artifact serialization failure dropped the
327	            # #G-009 artifact silently — an operator auditing the run saw no artifact and no reason.
328	            _log.warning(
329	                "experiment tracker call failed (%s: %s); this metric/artifact was not logged",
330	                type(exc).__name__,
331	                exc,
332	            )

346	        )

content.text:
This confirms my analysis. `_log_trail` runs inside `track()`, which is explicitly **best-effort observability** — a tracker artifact is decorative, never the source of truth. `persist_trail` is the **durable, re-verifiable** path with a documented round-trip contract.

I have enough to render a verdict.

input.reasoning:
The finding's mechanical observations are accurate (I reproduced both): pipeline.py:756-758 _log_trail uses json.dumps(..., default=str) while pipeline.py:240-243 persist_trail uses json.dumps() with no default, and ProvenanceTrail.record accepts hashable-but-non-JSON leaves (datetime/bytes), so the persist_trail path raises TypeError while the _log_trail path succeeds on the identical payload. But the finding's core thesis — that persist_trail is the "broken primary persistence path" that should be fixed to match _log_trail's "successful" behavior — is wrong, and the framing inverts which path is correct.

Decisive test: the default=str artifact _log_trail writes does NOT survive the documented round-trip. Reloading it via from_records/verify_records fails with "content was altered ... (hash mismatch)" — because _digest is computed over the original in-memory bytes/datetime payload, while default=str lossily stringifies those leaves (datetime -> '2020-01-01 00:00:00+00:00', b'secret' -> "b'secret'"). So _log_trail produces a GREEN-BUT-CORRUPTED, non-verifiable artifact. persist_trail raising TypeError (failing closed) on the same input is the CORRECT behavior for a durable cryptographic audit trail whose docstring promises re-verifiability via from_records/verify_records — a trail that cannot be exactly serialized must not be silently written in a form that won't verify.

The two paths SHOULD diverge by design: track()/_log_trail is explicitly best-effort observability ("a tracker failure never aborts a run"; failures are caught, recorded, and warned), an optional decorative artifact attached to an optional experiment tracker — never the source of truth. persist_trail is the durable audit path. There is no documented contract that they produce identical bytes. The K-OBS-2 fix's actual purpose (verified in the comment at 748-758) was narrow and legitimate: stop a serialization TypeError from (a) orphaning a NamedTemporaryFile and (b) silently dropping the #G-009 artifact with no operator signal — it now warns. That is a real best-effort-observability hardening, unrelated to the durable-trail contract.

The "sensitive bytes now land stringified" sub-claim is also weak: production internal record() calls (pipeline.py:315/339/etc.) pass only JSON-safe scalars; the only vector for a non-JSON leaf is operator-supplied gate evidence, and even then the artifact is lossy/non-authoritative observability. No real production defect: the divergence is intentional (lossy best-effort artifact vs. fail-closed durable audit trail), and persist_trail's raise is correct, not broken.

content.text:
I've completed my adversarial review of finding P9-REG-2 and returned the verdict via StructuredOutput.

Verdict: REFUTED

The finding's mechanical claims check out (I reproduced both the `default=str` divergence and `record()` accepting datetime/bytes leaves), but its thesis inverts which path is correct. The decisive evidence: the `_log_trail` artifact written with `default=str` does NOT survive the documented `from_records`/`verify_records` round-trip — it fails with a hash mismatch because the digest is computed over the original payload, not the stringified one. So `_log_trail` produces a green-but-non-verifiable artifact, while `persist_trail` raising `TypeError` (failing closed) is the correct behavior for a durable cryptographic audit trail. The two paths diverge by design: best-effort observability artifact vs. fail-closed durable audit. Not a production defect.
